text: This player receives rewards from others on several occasions but never visibly reciprocates with reward spending of their own. Their behavior after being rewarded does not shift into more generous or more stable cooperation; they continue their pattern of mixed, conditional contributions, sometimes matching the group and sometimes free‑riding. This suggests that they experience rewards more as a welcome payoff boost than as a binding social obligation. There is no clear evidence that they attempt to build bilateral alliances with specific rewarders: they do not start selectively rewarding or consistently matching the contributions of those who rewarded them. Their primary response channel remains their own contribution choice, and even that does not change in a strongly reciprocal way following received rewards. Overall, they are weakly responsive to positive reinforcement in terms of social reciprocity and alliance‑building; their dominant orientation remains individual payoff optimization with only limited, noisy responsiveness to others’ supportive actions.

text: When rewarded, the player tends to maintain or increase their own cooperative behavior, suggesting a positive response to recognition. They continue to contribute at high or at least substantial levels in subsequent play after receiving rewards, and they do not retaliate or withdraw cooperation in response to how rewards are allocated by others. This indicates that they interpret being rewarded as confirmation that cooperation is noticed and valued, rather than as a signal to exploit. However, there is no clear evidence that they reciprocate reward directly to the specific individual who rewarded them; any reciprocation is either diffuse (cooperating more with the group as a whole) or unknown. They do not demonstrate a simple one-to-one “you rewarded me, I will reward you” pattern. Thus, their reciprocity is more behavioral (sustained contributions) than monetary (return reward). They appear tolerant of uneven reward distributions. Even in periods when they contribute more than some peers and are not always rewarded proportionally, they do not systematically reduce contributions solely because of perceived reward unfairness. This suggests that their social style is relatively robust: they appreciate rewards and respond constructively to them, but they do not hinge their entire strategy on being rewarded perfectly. Overall, the player behaves like a conditional cooperator who uses reward as a tool to support prosocial partners, responds positively to being rewarded by further cooperation, but does not enforce strict dyadic reciprocity in the reward channel itself.

text: The player receives reward multiple times from others, sometimes in contexts where they themselves have contributed strongly and sometimes when they are free-riding. Despite this, they never respond by rewarding in return. There is no observable direct reciprocity toward rewarders, nor indirect reciprocity toward cooperators more generally. Thus, gratitude signaling via the reward tool is absent. Their contribution behavior after being rewarded does not show a consistent upward or downward shift tied clearly to the identity of the rewarder; given the complexity of the sequence, any subtle targeted reciprocity pattern is at most low-confidence and not clearly supported by the data. The safest inference is that while they may notice rewards (unknown), they do not convert that information into systematic changes in how they treat specific others. They do not attempt to form identifiable alliances, reward circles, or “mutual support” pairs through the reward mechanism. Overall, their response to being rewarded is instrumentally detached: they accept the positive payoff increment but do not alter their core strategic stance. Being rewarded does not move them from a cost-averse, self-regarding baseline toward costly pro-social actions.

text: This player is observably responsive to being rewarded. When they receive reward, they are willing to maintain or step up cooperative and reward-giving behavior in nearby rounds rather than exploiting the goodwill purely for personal gain. Their subsequent actions often continue to include sizable contributions and, at times, their own outgoing rewards, suggesting that they interpret incoming reward as a signal of mutual support rather than as a one-off windfall. They do not show clear targeted reciprocity (such as exclusively or obviously rewarding the specific individual who rewarded them), because the transcript does not identify recipients of their rewards; thus, whether they form explicit bilateral alliances is unknown. However, the combination of being willing to both give and receive rewards and to keep cooperating after being rewarded indicates that they are open to emergent cooperation clusters and are not suspicious of rewarders. In social-personality terms, they present as a conditional collaborator who uses both contribution choices and reward expenditures to explore and stabilize cooperative relationships, while still retaining a significant opportunistic streak and no strict aversion to free-riding when conditions look unfavorable.

text: The player is repeatedly rewarded by others, sometimes even in rounds where they contribute relatively little. Despite this, they never reciprocate with rewards and do not noticeably upgrade their contributions in a stable way toward the most generous peers. Rewarders are identifiable, so they have full information about who is treating them kindly. Nonetheless, they offer no direct reciprocity, neither in the reward stage nor through clear, sustained contribution increases toward group norms. Their behavior suggests gratitude (if present) does not translate into measurable costly actions. They do not appear to engage in alliance-building with generous partners via mutual rewarding. Overall, they are a non-reciprocating recipient of rewards: they accept benefits but maintain their original low-contribution and zero-reward stance, with only a late one-off shift to full contribution whose connection to received rewards is uncertain.

Their own contribution pattern does not visibly jump in direct response to being rewarded, and they do not shift their enforcement stance toward the players who reward them. They neither build an explicit cooperative alliance with rewarders nor acknowledge them through matching rewards. Their main response channel to social behavior remains punishment of under-contributors, not reciprocation of kindness.

text: When this player is rewarded, they do not reciprocate with reward in return, and they do not obviously tailor their later contributions to favor the specific rewarder. Their cooperation level remains conditionally responsive to the group as a whole rather than to dyadic relationships. They appear to interpret received rewards as incidental bonuses rather than as obligations or social signals requiring response. There is no evidence of alliance building, special protection, or special generosity toward those who reward them. Their stance reflects a relatively individualistic, non-relational use of the game’s mechanisms: they adapt to aggregate behavior, not to interpersonal gestures.

text: They are rewarded once by a highly cooperative, high‑spending peer but do not reciprocate with reward toward that peer in any later round. Nor do they noticeably adjust their contribution upward specifically in response to receiving that reward; their subsequent behavior includes both full contributions and substantial reductions. This absence of direct reciprocity toward an identifiable rewarder indicates that gratitude‑based or alliance‑building reciprocation is weak or absent. They do not appear to form a “coalition” with rewarders, nor do they differentiate in their sanction or reward use based on who previously treated them kindly. Their response suggests a relatively individualistic, strategy‑driven approach where external gestures of kindness have little systematic impact on their own sanctioning or reward decisions.

text: They show explicit verbal gratitude when acknowledged by others for their rewards and respond positively to appreciation. When they themselves receive rewards, they do not explicitly comment in chat, so their emotional response is unknown, but their sustained generous rewarding suggests they interpret reward signals as reinforcing and as part of a mutual prosocial relationship. They do not clearly engage in direct dyadic reciprocity (e.g., tightly targeted “you rewarded me so I reward only you”), but they do maintain generous reward behavior toward other active rewarders and contributors over time. This implies a soft reciprocity pattern: rewards from others strengthen their general prosocial rewarding orientation, but they do not narrowly condition on who rewarded them in the last round. Their priority appears to be maintaining a supportive atmosphere rather than constructing explicit reward-based alliances.

text: When others reward them, they acknowledge it explicitly and positively at least once, addressing rewarders by name and expressing appreciation. This indicates that they perceive reward as a social gesture rather than a purely instrumental transfer. They show some reciprocation through their own reward allocations: they reward players who are both cooperative and kind, which likely overlaps with those who have rewarded them, though exact reciprocity strength is uncertain. They do not overtly construct explicit bilateral “alliances” based on mutual rewarding, but they do signal affiliation and gratitude, which could support informal coalition structures. They never criticize or shame rewarders for “wasting” coins, implying that they normatively approve of using rewards as a social/prosocial tool even when it reduces earnings. Their stance toward rewarders is appreciative and relational; they treat receiving reward as confirmation of shared cooperative norms and good will.

text: They receive frequent rewards from others but never reciprocate. They do not respond via chat, do not mirror reward patterns, and do not appear to form reciprocal dyads or alliances. Their payoff gains from being rewarded also do not lead to any change in their own reward behavior; they remain a pure receiver of others’ generosity. This is consistent with a non-reciprocal, non-relational stance: they accept benefits without updating towards “rewarding back” those who are kind to them. It is unknown whether this reflects indifference, gratitude that they simply do not express, or a belief that contributions alone are the appropriate way to “give back.” There is no evidence that they track who rewards them in order to favor or disfavor those players later; their decisions stay invariant.

text: When identified others spend on rewarding them, this player reliably reciprocates over time. They direct substantial rewards toward those who have previously rewarded them and toward consistent cooperators, suggesting they interpret incoming rewards as signals of alliance or mutual support and respond in kind. This reciprocity appears to be relational rather than purely payoff-maximizing: they keep reciprocating even when the marginal strategic advantage of further reward is limited, implying that they treat mutual rewarding as an ongoing exchange norm. They do not verbally thank or acknowledge rewarders, but their allocation behavior functions as a nonverbal “thank you” and alliance reinforcement. There is no evidence that they retaliate against non-rewarders; they simply favor those who have rewarded them and contributed cooperatively.

There is no observable verbal gratitude or strategic recognition of rewarders in chat. Their contribution pattern also does not appear to systematically adjust as a direct response to being rewarded; they sometimes contribute fully after being rewarded and sometimes defect after being rewarded, without a clear contingent pattern. This suggests that they treat incoming rewards as “free money” rather than socially meaningful signals. They do not appear to interpret rewarding as something that binds them into an alliance or obligation. Their stance toward rewarders is effectively exploitative: they benefit from rewards while giving nothing back, neither materially nor socially.

text: When others reward them, this player typically responds with continued or renewed high cooperation and with sustained willingness to reward in future rounds. Their behavior suggests gratitude-driven reciprocity: receiving reward does not lead them to reduce contributions; instead, they either maintain full cooperation or, in many cases, continue to spend on rewarding others in subsequent rounds. They appear to interpret being rewarded as confirmation of a cooperative alliance and respond by reinforcing that alliance. They neither exploit the rewarders by slacking off nor drop reward once their own position improves. Instead, they behave as though reward establishes a mutual obligation to keep supporting each other and the group. Over time, this pattern indicates that the player uses identifiable rewards as a basis for building and sustaining cooperative relationships, and their response is to be a reliable, generous partner rather than a purely strategic opportunist.

text: The player does receive rewards multiple times, including in situations where they contribute less than their peers, yet they never change their reward behavior in response. They do not start rewarding once they are rewarded, do not single out any visible reciprocation pattern, and do not visibly adjust contributions in a manner that clearly targets specific rewarders versus non-rewarders (individual-level targeting is not inferable from the data). This suggests that they treat received rewards as a pleasant but non-strategic windfall, not as a cue that obliges reciprocity. Being able to identify rewarders does not trigger alliance-building behavior through the reward channel. The player does not initiate reciprocal rewarding or coalition-like behavior. If they respond to rewarders at all, it is indirectly through maintaining reasonably high contributions that keep the cooperative environment intact, but this is indistinguishable from their general cooperative baseline and cannot be conclusively tied to gratitude. Their social style toward rewarders is thus “non-reciprocating but not hostile”: they accept rewards, do not punish or reduce contributions in response, but also do not invest in returning favors. They appear to separate private payoff optimization (keeping their coins) from social signaling, letting others bear the cost of rewarding while they remain strategically aloof on that dimension.

text: When this player is rewarded, they do not shift into a retaliatory or exploitative stance. Instead, their cooperative pattern continues or intensifies, and they keep rewarding others as long as high contributions persist. This suggests that they interpret incoming rewards as confirmation of a cooperative alliance and respond by reinforcing that alliance, rather than by free-riding on others’ continued generosity. They show no evidence of withdrawing reward from cooperators who reward them less, and there is no clear pattern of selectively targeting only those who reward them. This implies limited concern with strict dyadic reciprocity in rewards; their standard seems to be “reward good contributors” more than “reward those who reward me.” They appear comfortable occupying the role of a consistent prosocial rewarder, even when they themselves are not the primary beneficiary of others’ rewards. Overall, their response to being rewarded is to maintain contribution, maintain or increase rewarding activity, and continue signaling support for cooperative partners. This points toward a personality that values sustaining cooperative norms and group welfare over maximizing their own net payoff from the reward mechanism, and toward a belief that stable cooperation is best supported by visible, consistent positive reinforcement rather than by conditional, tit-for-tat exchanges of reward.

text: The player is responsive to being rewarded but not in a simple tit-for-tat sense. When they receive reward, they sometimes also spend to reward others, but they also reward in rounds where their own received rewards are low or absent. This pattern suggests that they are not merely reciprocating directly to whoever rewards them; instead, they behave as a generally prosocial type whose reward decisions are driven more by others’ contribution levels and the state of cooperation than by personal gratitude repayment. There is no clear evidence of tight bilateral alliances or targeted reciprocity; any such patterns are unknown. Their consistent full contribution despite fluctuations in how much they are rewarded implies that their cooperative behavior does not depend on being appreciated or compensated. Being rewarded does not make them more cooperative (they are already at the maximum), but it does not cause frustration or withdrawal when rewards are low. Overall, they behave like a cooperative norm enforcer who appreciates rewards but does not hinge their strategy on forming exclusive exchange relationships.

Their main response to being rewarded is indirect: they maintain relatively cooperative contributions rather than sharply reducing them, but this behavior is not distinctly tied to who rewarded them. There is no evidence of alliance-building via mutual rewarding or of targeted gratitude; they appear not to track or prioritize “who helped me” in a way that changes their own reward decisions. Their reciprocity is therefore weak or absent in the explicit reward mechanism, and likely expressed—if at all—only through continued group-level cooperation, not dyadic exchange.

text: This player often receives rewards from others but never reciprocates via the reward mechanism. They do not change their reward behavior from zero even after being rewarded multiple times, including by specific, identifiable others. There is no evidence that they form reciprocal reward alliances or selectively reinforce players who have previously rewarded them. Instead, their cooperative behavior (high contributions) continues largely independent of who is rewarding them. This indicates very low use of direct reciprocity in the reward stage. Their contributions also remain high even in periods when reward to them is minimal or absent, implying that they do not treat being rewarded as a necessary condition for cooperation, nor as a signal that shifts their strategy. They appear to treat incoming rewards as incidental benefits rather than as part of a relationship-building process that requires reciprocation.

text: The player does not display clear, consistent direct reciprocity toward specific identifiable rewarders. They receive rewards from different others at various times, including when they themselves contribute nothing. Their own subsequent rewarding does not reliably target those benefactors, nor does it consistently increase after being rewarded. Sometimes they continue or resume rewarding after having been rewarded, which may reflect a diffuse sense of mutual support, but because targets are not identifiable in the summary, their precise reciprocity pattern is unknown. What is clear is that the player does not “lock in” to alliances where they consistently favor those who have rewarded them; their reward behavior remains volatile, suggesting that immediate, dyadic reciprocity is at most a weak driver. The player also shows no strong evidence of “moral cleansing” after receiving rewards when free‑riding (e.g., they do not systematically switch to high contributions and heavy rewarding immediately after being rewarded as a low contributor). Any such effects appear sporadic. This points to a type that appreciates favorable treatment but does not feel compelled to repay it in a structured or predictable way.

text: This player receives rewards on several occasions from others, but they do not establish clear reciprocal reward relationships. There is no stable pattern where they repeatedly reward those who have rewarded them in the past, nor do they shift to higher contributions in a durable way after being rewarded. If reciprocity exists, it is weak and inconsistent. They do not appear to form identifiable “alliances” based on mutual rewarding: no clear tit-for-tat reward cycles or sustained mutual support emerge from their behavior. Their modest contribution increases around some rewarded periods are not reliable enough to be interpreted as a strong gratitude response. Instead, they treat incoming rewards largely as windfall gains: welcome extra payoff, not a signal that obliges them to invest back into the relationship or the group norm. In belief terms, they likely interpret rewards as noise or as something driven by others’ idiosyncrasies rather than as a stable contract. Their response indicates a low propensity toward costly reciprocal thanking behavior and a preference to keep their own strategy relatively independent of who has rewarded them.

text: The player receives small, positive rewards in several rounds from multiple peers but does not initially reciprocate with rewards. Their contributions do not sharply increase immediately in response to being rewarded; instead, they remain in a moderate-to-high cooperative band that appears more tied to the overall contribution environment than to specific acts of kindness toward them. Only once, near the end, do they use rewards themselves, and they distribute them equally to all rather than preferentially to their past rewarders. This behavior implies low sensitivity to personalized reciprocity via rewards and a limited interest in forming bilateral “alliances” based on mutual gifting. They treat rewards as a pleasant but non-decisive feature of the environment, continuing to cooperate at a system level rather than engaging in targeted payback. Their response style is thus steady and group-oriented, not strongly driven by gratitude or tit-for-tat rewarding.

Their reaction is purely instrumental—they integrate the extra income into their payoff but do not treat reward as a social obligation or a cue for targeted reciprocity. At the same time, they do not reduce contributions or exploit rewarders, so their response is best described as neutral and unresponsive rather than opportunistically exploitative. Overall, this player is a “quiet, payoff-conscious conditional cooperator”: they mostly follow cooperative norms, adjust upward after punishment, avoid all sanctioning costs themselves, and stay socially disengaged, using others’ communication and sanctions to guide their own contributions without investing in enforcement or relationship-building.

text: This player is responsive to being rewarded, but not in a simple, mechanical way. They do not reciprocate with immediate, guaranteed reward back to those who reward them, nor do they always significantly change their contribution after every instance of receiving reward. Yet their overall pattern shows a tendency to maintain or adopt cooperative levels when they experience supportive behavior from others. When rewarded after cooperative behavior, they often continue to contribute at moderate-to-high levels, indicating that reward helps stabilize their cooperative stance rather than triggering drastic positive or negative shifts. They do not use the reward channel to construct clear bilateral “alliances” (e.g., consistent mutual rewarding back-and-forth), at least not reliably enough to infer a deliberate tit-for-tat reward strategy. Instead, their main response to reward appears to be at the level of general norm reinforcement: being rewarded is consistent with their inclination to keep cooperating, and they occasionally reciprocate at the group level by continuing to invest in the public good and by selectively rewarding cooperators in later situations. Their reciprocity is thus diffuse and behavior-based (supporting cooperators) rather than specifically directed at named individuals in a systematic pattern. Personality-wise, this suggests that they appreciate recognition, are not vengeful or tightly score-keeping, and prefer to respond to “good behavior” with continued good behavior and occasional reward, rather than treating reward exchanges as strict interpersonal contracts.

While the transcript does not allow a precise mapping of one-to-one reciprocal transfers, the aggregate pattern suggests that they are more likely to reward those who both contribute and participate in the reward system, and less likely to support clear non-rewarding free-riders. This indicates a tendency toward positive reciprocity and alliance-like thinking: they distinguish between supportive and nonsupportive partners and appear to build a loose coalition of cooperators through targeted reward. When rewards to them decrease later, they do not retaliate or sharply reduce contributions toward specific others, but they do reduce their own reward spending overall, which aligns more with strategic adjustment than emotional retaliation. Their reciprocity is thus present but primarily strategic and forward-looking.

This pattern indicates that they treat received reward as a windfall rather than as a relationship cue or obligation; they do not appear to internalize it as something that should be reciprocated or reinforced. Their reciprocity via the explicit reward channel is effectively absent. If they feel gratitude, it is not expressed behaviorally in this game. Their subsequent return to cooperation appears driven more by general group dynamics or personal preference than by loyalty to specific rewarders.

There is no indication they interpret rewards as the beginning of an alliance; they treat it as background and continue with their prior strategy. text: The player is themselves rewarded several times by others, and they clearly see who rewards them. There is partial but inconsistent positive reciprocity: they sometimes respond by rewarding those same others in subsequent rounds or by praising them in chat, but at other times they later punish or free-ride against those same individuals. Their responsiveness appears more tied to current emotional state or conversational dynamics than to a stable principle of “reward those who reward me.” They do not lock into strong alliances based on rewards received, nor do they systematically favor prior rewarders in their later punishment decisions. The evidence suggests that gratitude, if present, is short-lived and easily overridden by boredom, irritation, or a desire to mix things up.

text: They receive multiple rewards from others over the game, yet never mirror this behavior with reciprocal rewards. Nor do they verbally single out rewarders for thanks; their only explicit praise is directed to a player who changes from low to high contributions, not necessarily connected to being a rewarder. This unreciprocated acceptance suggests they do not interpret receiving rewards as an obligation to return them via the same mechanism. Instead, they respond by continuing to contribute at a high level and by maintaining a cooperative, supportive tone. They appear to value the cooperative structure itself more than the specific act of being materially rewarded. In terms of type, this reflects a “cooperate but not side-pay” stance: they accept rewards as confirmation that high contributions are appreciated, but they do not invest in reward networks or alliance-building via costly positive transfers.

text: Despite consistently being rewarded by others, this player gives no observable reciprocal response. They do not increase their contributions specifically toward rewarders, nor do they spend coins to reward those who have benefited them. In fact, their behavior indicates that receiving rewards does not meaningfully alter their contribution pattern beyond whatever general conditionality they already exhibit. They also do not appear to differentiate between players who repeatedly reward them and players who do not: there is no targeted reciprocity, coalition-building, or tit-for-tat style answering of kindness with costly kindness. Rewards received effectively function as “free money” rather than as social signals that elicit obligation or gratitude. This suggests a low propensity for reciprocity under identifiable reward conditions and a tendency to treat others’ reward behavior as exploitable rather than as something to be matched. In new games where rewarders are visible, they are likely to accept benefits but not adjust their strategy to favor or protect those rewarders.

text: This player appears sensitive to being rewarded and tends to reciprocate positively, but not in a narrowly transactional way. They often reward those who have rewarded them, which is consistent with reciprocity and alliance-building, yet they also allocate rewards to cooperative partners who have not necessarily just rewarded them, suggesting that “you cooperated” matters at least as much as “you rewarded me.” They do not fixate on any single partner; instead, they distribute rewards among multiple players across time, which fits a view of reward as a general social tool rather than a bilateral exchange. When they receive substantial rewards, they do not respond by sharply reducing contributions, indicating that they treat rewards as encouragement to stay cooperative rather than as a substitute for their own effort. Overall, they behave like a reciprocal cooperator: appreciative of reward, inclined to reward rewarders back when consistent with contribution patterns, but still anchored in contribution-based fairness rather than pure gift-exchange.

text: The player is responsive to being rewarded and appears to interpret reward as a meaningful social signal. When they receive reward, they do not retaliate or reduce contributions; instead, they continue to participate in the public good and to spend on rewarding others. There is some indication of positive reciprocity or alliance building: after periods where another player incurs significant reward costs, the focal player continues to show cooperative effort and remains active in the reward system, rather than exploiting the rewarders by cutting back contributions. However, there is no clear, tight one‑to‑one reciprocity rule (e.g., exactly matching reward back to specific individuals); individual‑level mapping from who rewarded them to whom they reward is unknown, and the precision of their reciprocity is low‑confidence. Overall, they seem socially responsive and appreciative of cooperative and rewarding partners, but they implement this through general reinforcement of cooperators rather than explicit dyadic tit‑for‑tat.

text: This player consistently receives sizable rewards from multiple partners but does not reciprocate via the reward mechanism. There is no behavioral evidence that they form a closed “reward alliance”; they accept rewards without mirroring them back to specific individuals. Their main “reciprocity,” if any, appears in their contribution decisions rather than reward spending: they often continue to contribute fully even when they are not uniquely favored by rewards, and they do not switch to rewarding those who reward them. This behavior suggests they may interpret rewards as confirmation that their cooperative strategy is appreciated, but they do not feel compelled to pay back in kind. If they reciprocate at all, it is indirect and muted. Their one large reward episode is not clearly targeted to prior personal benefactors in a proportional way, so any intent to reciprocate is uncertain. Personality-wise, they resemble a player who values being rewarded but treats the reward channel as secondary to contribution decisions, and who is comfortable benefiting from others’ generosity without tightly coupling their own reward behavior to it.

text: They are rewarded multiple times by others but never reciprocate via the formal reward mechanism. Despite knowing exactly who rewards them, they do not adopt a tit-for-tat in the reward dimension. Instead, their only observable “response” is through their subsequent contribution levels, which remain within their usual range and do not clearly lock into a distinct “all-in to allies” pattern after being rewarded. This suggests that gratitude, if present, is not expressed through costly positive reciprocity in this game. They may mentally appreciate the reward but treat it as a windfall rather than as a cue to invest in a bilateral alliance. Alternatively, they may view rewarders as irrationally sacrificing payoffs and so do not mirror that behavior. They appear to maintain a group-level strategy rather than shifting to dyadic favoritism: being rewarded does not produce special treatment toward any specific peer. In new environments with identifiable rewarders, they likely continue to focus on adjusting contributions at the group level, not on building or maintaining side relationships through the reward mechanism. Overall, they are a conditional cooperator with minimal use of formal reciprocity tools and low propensity to “pay back” favors when it is inefficient to do so.

text: This player receives reward only once, and the amount is small relative to the total opportunities in the game. After being rewarded, there is no clear change in their already-high contribution pattern, nor a visible shift in their reward spending strategy. They do not appear to initiate targeted reciprocal reward or alliance behavior in direct response to that event. Given that rewarders are identifiable, the absence of clear reciprocation suggests that their rewarding is driven more by others’ contribution levels than by who has personally rewarded them. In terms of inferred social style, they behave more like a principled, norm-based rewarder than a tit-for-tat reciprocator. They do not condition their cooperation or reward on getting something back; instead, they continue to cooperate and to reward based on their own standard of contribution quality, largely independent of how much they themselves are appreciated or compensated. Whether they emotionally register gratitude is unknown; behaviorally, they do not substantially adjust in a way that indicates personalized gratitude or alliance-building.

text: The player receives rewards multiple times and can see that others sometimes single them out for positive reinforcement. Being rewarded does not noticeably increase their willingness to reward back; they never reciprocate with reward use. Nor does it produce a durable escalation in their contribution beyond their usual personal standard. At most, they sometimes adjust their contribution around the same moderate range regardless of whether they were just rewarded. This indicates low behavioral reciprocity in the reward domain and limited propensity to form “reward alliances” even when the technology makes rewarders identifiable. However, the player does not appear to punish rewarders by reducing contributions; they remain reliably cooperative at their chosen level. Their reaction is best characterized as appreciative but not strategically responsive: they accept rewards, do not oppose them, but also do not modify their strategy to actively support or reciprocate them. Internally, this fits a type who values being seen as cooperative enough to attract rewards, but who separates that identity from any obligation to incur their own extra costs to reinforce others.

text: The player receives reward from others multiple times and reacts in a nuanced but self-centered way. Being rewarded does not lock them into unconditional cooperation; they still occasionally free-ride or reduce contributions after benefiting from others’ rewards. This shows that they do not interpret reward as creating a binding obligation to “repay” the group or specific partners. However, they also do not simply exploit rewarders relentlessly. After periods of being rewarded, they sometimes respond with higher or at least non-minimal contributions, which suggests a limited, probabilistic form of reciprocity or at least sensitivity to maintaining the relationship that is benefiting them. Their behavior is compatible with “soft gratitude”: reward nudges them toward cooperation at times, but it does not override their baseline payoff focus. There is no evidence that they actively identify and reward their rewarders in return, so any reciprocity is expressed mainly through contribution levels rather than through using the reward mechanism. They accept rewards and let them improve their payoff, adjusting their own contributions only when it also seems individually advantageous. In new PGG settings with identifiable rewarders, they are likely to appreciate and sometimes partially reciprocate supportive partners by not exploiting them maximally, but they are unlikely to incur large personal costs purely out of gratitude.

Instead, they repeatedly reward both main cooperative partners roughly symmetrically and often reward all active partners when everyone cooperates, regardless of who has directly benefited them in the past. Their own contributions are already high even before they receive any reward, and these remain high afterward, so any compliance effect is hard to detect; they are not using others’ rewards as cues to adjust effort upward. Their behavior is more consistent with a stable cooperative identity than with tit-for-tat reciprocity to reward. Their stable refusal to reward, even when they are a beneficiary of others’ generosity, suggests they do not interpret the reward channel as a reciprocal obligation. Instead, they treat being rewarded as a windfall or group-side effect that does not alter their own strategic obligations. They also do not shift their contributions in response to being rewarded; their contribution path is essentially independent of their reward inflows. Consequently, they exhibit low reciprocity via the optional reward mechanism, even though they are highly cooperative in the main PGG. This combination characterizes them as a “pure contributor” who cooperates through the main channel but does not engage in secondary, costly reciprocity when the cost–benefit ratio is unfavorable, and who does not use identifiable rewarding to manage relationships or signal gratitude in a targeted way.

They still remain fully cooperative, but that cooperation predates and does not change in response to being rewarded. This suggests their cooperation is intrinsic or norm-based rather than contingent on direct reciprocity for personal rewards. Their behavioral response to being rewarded is essentially neutral: no observable gratitude signaling, no strategic mutual-reward pattern, and no behavioral shift.

text: They receive rewards from others but never comment on them in chat and never reciprocate via the mechanism. There is no observable explicit gratitude, reciprocal rewarding, or coalition signaling toward those who reward them or contribute fully. Their behavior suggests that their cooperative strategy is not contingent on being rewarded and that they do not update their interpersonal stance based on receiving positive transfers. How they would behave in a setting where verbal thanks or explicit reciprocity is more salient is unknown; here they remain neutral and steady.

text: The player receives small rewards from others on at least one occasion but does not explicitly acknowledge or reciprocate them in the chat. They do not form explicit alliances based on reward exchange, nor do they change their rewarding or punishing behavior in response to being rewarded. Their reward behavior (when present) is more driven by their own early pro-social impulse than by specific reciprocation. Thus, they show low explicit reciprocity to identifiable rewarders and appear not to use rewards as a basis for coalition-building or preferential treatment. Any gratitude is implicit at best; their chat focuses on confusion, apologies, and humor rather than on thanking rewarders or promising future cooperation.

text: They receive substantial rewards from others early in the game but never comment on this explicitly and never reciprocate via rewards. This suggests that, while they accept the benefits, they do not interpret rewards as creating an obligation to reward back. They also do not verbally thank or highlight rewarders. Their behavior implies that rewards have little influence on their strategy: they continue to fully contribute, but that is already their baseline, and they do not change punishment or communication patterns in response. They are thus not a reciprocity-seeking type in the “reward for reward” sense, and do not appear to treat rewarders as special allies. Their primary axis of reciprocity is via contribution norms, not via the reward mechanism.

text: On at least two occasions, they receive rewards from others. Their subsequent behavior does not shift in any notable way in response: they neither increase their already high contributions nor begin using the reward mechanism themselves. Thus, they do not display observable reciprocal rewarding, even though rewarders are identifiable to recipients. Their contribution pattern after being rewarded remains consistent with their baseline strategy—high contributions with occasional moderate dips that also occur in periods with no reward. There is no detectable “gratitude spike” in contribution level uniquely associated with being rewarded, and no move to form a reciprocal reward alliance. This suggests that while they accept rewards, they do not internalize a norm of returning favors through the same channel. Psychologically, this is consistent with a type who appreciates cooperation but does not treat formal, costly reward as a behavior that must be reciprocated. Their reciprocity seems to be expressed (if at all) through continuing to contribute to the public good, not through targeted positive transfers. In strategic terms, they act as if being rewarded confirms that their cooperative strategy is acceptable but does not call for additional, costly actions toward the rewarder. Overall, this player is best characterized as a high, fairly steadfast contributor who is weakly responsive to end-game incentives, does not invest in formal rewards, and does not visibly reciprocate when rewarded, while still maintaining a generally cooperative stance throughout the interaction.

text: The player is rewarded at least once, but there is no visible change in their contribution pattern afterward, because they are already contributing at or near the maximum before being rewarded and continue to do so afterward. Thus, they do not escalate cooperation in response to being rewarded; their cooperative level is already saturated. They do engage in rewarding both before and after receiving a reward, so any “gratitude-driven” change in their reward behavior is either minimal or not clearly distinguishable from their existing selective-reward strategy. There is no clear evidence that they build an explicit reciprocal “alliance” with a particular high contributor via concentrated rewards; instead, their rewarding remains sparse and tied to high overall group cooperation rather than specific dyadic reciprocity. Overall, they appear steady and not especially reactive: being rewarded does not visibly alter their risk-taking, contribution levels, or general attitude toward the group.

text: When another player rewards them, their behavioral response is notably inert: they do not change contributions, do not start rewarding more frequently, and do not display obvious reciprocity through the reward channel. Their already-maximal contributions leave little room for increasing cooperation as a reaction, and they do not shift into active reward-giving in apparent response to being rewarded. This suggests that their cooperative stance is driven more by internal norms or a stable strategy than by direct reciprocity to reward signals. Because rewarders are identifiable, the player could build explicit alliances but does not visibly do so; their later, isolated use of reward appears aimed at high cooperators in general rather than clearly targeted reciprocal payback to the specific prior rewarder (this is uncertain, as identities for that action are unknown). The safest inference is that they display weak or muted reciprocity in the reward dimension: they appreciate or at least accept reward without retaliation or escalation, but it does not materially alter their strategy. If placed in a new PGG, they continue cooperating at a high level whether or not they receive rewards, and any reward they give remains more norm-reinforcing than tit-for-tat.

text: The player receives a reward once, clearly seeing that someone has spent their own resources to benefit them. There is no subsequent change in their use of the reward mechanism: they continue not to reward anyone. This indicates that they do not reciprocate materially via the formal reward option, even when they personally benefit from another’s generosity. Their contributions after being rewarded remain within their usual range, showing no strong evidence of emotional gratitude translated into sustained higher cooperation. Any local fluctuations are indistinguishable from their general pattern of medium-to-variable contributions. There is no sign of alliance-building behavior directed toward a specific rewarder; if they infer who rewarded them, they do not visibly change strategy toward that individual through rewards. Psychologically, this pattern fits a type who appreciates benefits privately but does not internalize a strong obligation to “give back” when such giving is personally costly. Their main lever for social adjustment remains their own contribution level, not the use of institutional reward tools. In sum, they are relatively non-reciprocal in the explicit reward channel and treat being rewarded as a windfall rather than a cue for active reciprocation.

text: They are repeatedly and heavily rewarded by several other players across the game, in both low- and high-contribution phases. Despite this, they never reciprocate by rewarding their benefactors or anyone else. Their reaction to being rewarded is purely instrumental: they accept increased payoffs but do not attempt to build alliances, reciprocate kindness, or stabilize cooperation through reward. Any adaptation they show—moving from free-riding toward cooperation—does not manifest as “rewarding back,” but rather as changing their own contribution behavior within the public good. This suggests they view rewards more as windfall gains than as obligations or social cues requiring a direct “in-kind” response.

Nevertheless, they do align their contributions with the cooperative norm that those same players appear to support, suggesting that rewards may help stabilize their cooperative behavior even if they do not reciprocate via the same mechanism. There is no evidence of forming explicit “alliances” via mutual reward; their behavior toward rewarders remains identical to their behavior toward other high contributors: they refrain from punishing them and continue to contribute at the same high level. Their response to being rewarded is thus behavioral compliance with group norms but not direct reciprocal generosity.

text: They receive substantial rewards across the game but never reciprocate via reward-giving. This non-reciprocity suggests that gratitude, if felt, does not translate into costly prosocial action. They are willing to adjust their contribution behavior toward full cooperation later, which may be partly influenced by being included in generous reward patterns, but the connection between who rewards them and how they respond is opaque. Their response to being rewarded is thus primarily behavioral in contribution (eventual alignment with cooperative norms), not in direct repayment. This fits a type that appreciates the benefits of a cooperative, reward-rich environment but prefers to be a passive beneficiary rather than an active contributor to the reward system.

text: When rewarded, they do not single out rewarders for special treatment. Players who reward them and those who do not are both included in their broad reward patterns. They sometimes reward others who have previously rewarded them, but they also reward those who have not, and they even generously reward players who have punished them. Because their reward is distributed so widely and generously, it is difficult to distinguish targeted reciprocity from generalized altruism. There is no clear evidence of forming “alliances” in which they concentrate benefits primarily on mutual reward partners. Any reciprocity motivation, if present, is weak and overshadowed by a general tendency to give to everyone.

text: They are frequently rewarded by other players, often to a substantial extent, but they never reciprocate through rewards or punishment, and they never adjust their contribution (which is already maximal). Their response to being rewarded is thus purely behavioral inertia: they continue their full contributions, as they already did before receiving any rewards. They do not appear to use rewards as a signal to join a “coalition” of rewarders, nor do they selectively reinforce those who treat them generously. There is no observable strategic alliance-building; they accept rewards but do not mirror them. This suggests either that they treat rewards as incidental and not as a cue for changed strategy, or that they adhere to a fixed norm (“I just always contribute fully; that is my part”) and do not see reward-giving as part of their role.

text: They receive rewards from several others, including strong cooperators and a player who spends heavily on rewarding. After being rewarded, they do not change their contribution pattern (which is already maximally cooperative), but they do evolve their sanctioning pattern: they shift away from heavy punishment toward a more reward-focused behavior once the environment is cooperative. While there is no simple one-to-one reciprocation to every individual rewarder, their later rewards disproportionately go toward players who both cooperate and themselves engage in prosocial rewarding. This indicates a tendency toward generalized reciprocity or alliance-building with other “prosocial enforcers,” rather than strict tit-for-tat. They respond to being rewarded not by exploiting others’ generosity but by continuing and even reinforcing the cooperative, norm-supporting environment.

text: This player notices when they receive rewards and appears responsive, but not in a simple tit‑for‑tat manner. After being rewarded, they do not immediately switch into costless reciprocation via the reward system, even though identities are known. Instead, their main form of reciprocity is through sustained or increased contributions to the public good when they perceive a cooperative and rewarding partner in the group. Their behavior suggests they interpret being rewarded as confirmation that cooperative norms are shared by at least one other player, which encourages them to maintain or raise their own cooperative stance rather than to directly “pay back” the reward cost. This points to an indirect reciprocity strategy: they repay rewarders by supporting overall group efficiency and by continuing to be a reliable contributor, particularly when that partner is also contributing strongly. There is no clear evidence that they preferentially reward the specific individual who rewarded them, although this is uncertain given the absence of explicit attribution in the record. What is clearer is that receiving reward does not trigger retaliation, withdrawal, or opportunistic exploitation; instead, it is met with stable or higher cooperation and continued use of the reward mechanism toward cooperators in general. Personality‑wise, this is consistent with someone who values mutual support and group norms more than narrow dyadic bookkeeping: they respond positively to being valued but channel that response into maintaining a cooperative environment rather than into strict one‑to‑one repayment.

text: The player receives rewards multiple times from others, often after they themselves contribute at least moderately or align with cooperative behavior. Their own rewarding behavior does not systematically escalate after being rewarded, and there is no clear pattern of direct reciprocity to specific individuals. text: When they receive rewards, they do not respond with strong, consistent reciprocity toward the rewarders. Being rewarded does not reliably trigger an increase in their own contributions or in their own use of the reward mechanism that would clearly target those who rewarded them. They sometimes continue to free-ride after being rewarded and also sometimes continue to reward others without clear evidence of reciprocating specific individuals. This suggests that they do not treat reward as the basis for a tight alliance with particular players. Instead, they appear to interpret rewards more diffusely—as a general signal that cooperation and reward-giving are “on the table” in the group—without converting that into clear dyadic reciprocity. Their behavior is compatible with a player who appreciates being rewarded but does not feel strongly obligated to return the favor in a targeted way, or who struggles to track and implement specific tit-for-tat-like responses. In new settings, this player is likely to notice and generally value being rewarded, but their behavioral adjustment will be broad and inconsistent rather than precise: they might slightly increase cooperative or rewarding tendencies overall, yet they will not reliably form stable reciprocal bonds with identifiable rewarders.

text: The player receives reward in multiple rounds from others, but their response is purely at the level of aggregate strategy: they do not take up rewarding themselves, and they do not clearly reciprocate to the specific identities who reward them (which would require sending rewards back, which never happens). Instead, after being rewarded, they continue using the same two-level contribution pattern, sometimes contributing fully and sometimes at a mid level, with no clear step change linked to getting rewarded. Because the identities of rewarders are visible, their lack of targeted reciprocation suggests they prioritize their own global rule (“cooperate conditionally via contributions”) over dyadic reciprocity through the reward channel. They do not appear to interpret reward as a strong coordination signal requiring special loyalty to the rewarder. Gratitude, if present, is expressed only indirectly by maintaining relatively high contributions that benefit the group as a whole rather than by selectively favoring allies. Overall, they behave as a conditional cooperator who appreciates cooperative environments but does not engage in explicit alliance-building or relational bookkeeping via the reward mechanism.

text: They are rewarded multiple times by identifiable players but do not reciprocate with rewards or visible strategic deference. They do not verbally acknowledge the rewards and do not alter their subsequent reward behavior from zero. text: When rewarded, they notice and show appreciation in chat toward those who reward or otherwise support them. They sometimes explicitly thank other players and express warmth toward those who act generously or back their punishment campaigns. They also occasionally reciprocate by rewarding apparent allies, though this reciprocity is not purely one-to-one and may be more symbolic than strictly contingent. They treat rewarders as part of their in-group, praising them, joking with them, and defending cooperative norms alongside them. There is no evidence of them turning against those who reward them; alliances once formed appear stable through the game. Being rewarded does not obviously change their contribution pattern—they are already frequently giving high amounts in many of these interactions—but it reinforces their social connection with the rewarding players. Overall, their response to reward is affiliative and appreciative. They use it to reinforce social bonds and shared identity as “good players” in contrast to the stigmatized low contributors.

text: They receive rewards multiple times from others, particularly from a consistently generous player. They do not explicitly single out their rewarders with thanks in the chat log, but they do later join in praising that generous player and acknowledging their special role in the group, which may be a form of indirect reciprocity. They do not reciprocate with monetary rewards, indicating that gratitude is expressed symbolically and socially rather than materially. However, they maintain maximum contributions and strong norm enforcement, which can be interpreted as generalized reciprocity toward the cooperative group rather than targeted reciprocity toward individual rewarders. Alliances are built via shared norms and shared targets (punishing defectors) rather than via mutual gifting. Overall, this player type is a sociable, verbally expressive, high-contribution enforcer: they support cooperative norms with their own contributions, actively coordinate group behavior, and rely heavily on costly punishment—rather than rewards—to police and retaliate against perceived free riders or saboteurs.

text: They are attentive to who rewards them and openly express gratitude and affection toward those allies. When rewarded by a consistently generous player, they publicly thank them and later reciprocate with sizable rewards and positive labels, reinforcing a cooperative alliance. They also verbally support and defend those high-reward, high-contribution players in chat, helping to solidify their positive reputation. This reciprocity is not strictly tit-for-tat in magnitude but is clearly directional: being rewarded increases their affinity toward the rewarder and their willingness to expend their own coins to benefit that person. text: They receive some rewards from others, including from the player they repeatedly support, but there is no clear evidence that they adjust their behavior materially in response to being rewarded. They do not explicitly thank rewarders in chat or publicly acknowledge them as allies, and they do not exhibit a clear pattern of reciprocal, one-for-one reward to those who give them gifts. Their alliance with the other low contributor appears to be initiated and maintained more by their own preference than by reciprocity to that player’s earlier actions. When they reward other cooperative players (such as a widely praised contributor), the motive seems more like occasional positive signaling than repayment for specific gifts. Overall, their reciprocity is coarse-grained and coalition-based rather than fine-grained and tit-for-tat: they reward those they see as “their side” or as deserving, not necessarily those who reward them, and they do not appear to use reward as a tool to cultivate a broad network of mutual supporters. --- Type label: “Loyal Rebel with Costly Solidarity” Summary of type: This player is not a standard selfish free-rider nor a straightforward cooperator-enforcer. They show an early inclination to exploit a cooperative group, but once they are singled out and punished, they pivot toward a distinctive strategy: partial norm compliance combined with extremely costly support for a stigmatized peer and broad retaliatory sanctions against the dominant coalition. They care far less about maximizing their own earnings than about expressing loyalty, resisting group pressure, and sending strong signals about whom they stand with. In new PGG environments, expect them to: - Experiment with under-contributing relative to cooperative norms. - Become defensive and oppositional if targeted by moralistic or aggressive punishments. - Devote large resources to rewarding allies and punishing perceived bullies, even when it severely harms their own payoff. - Partially adjust contributions when heavily sanctioned, but maintain a rebellious identity and preference for coalition-based rather than system-based fairness.

text: They do receive some rewards during the game but never explicitly name or thank particular rewarders, and they do not visibly build dyadic alliances based on being rewarded. When others are praised for rewarding (e.g., a highly generous rewarder is celebrated), they join in that positive narrative indirectly (by rewarding that player at least once) but not in an overt “you scratched my back, I’ll scratch yours” manner. This pattern indicates that while they are sensitive to social acknowledgment, they prioritize the overall cooperative norm and group outcome over personal reward-based alliances.

text: They receive rewards from several players across the game but never explicitly acknowledge them in chat. There is no direct verbal reciprocity toward specific rewarders. However, their heavy mid-game reward to a highly generous and rewarding cooperator suggests indirect reciprocity: they favor someone widely viewed as generous, which likely includes appreciation for prior rewards (even if they do not say so). In the final round, they send small rewards to nearly all co-players, including individuals who have previously punished them. This broad generosity extends even to some players whose earlier behavior was mixed. That pattern is consistent with a forgiving or at least forward-looking orientation: they do not use the final opportunity to settle old scores or withhold benefits from those who hurt them, but instead adopt a “farewell gift” approach. Their behavioral response to rewarders is thus mostly non-verbal, expressed through prosocial acts toward the group rather than explicit thanks or one-to-one repayment. Overall, this player type can be summarized as a **quiet prosocial enforcer**: mostly high-contributing, strongly willing to punish defectors, unusually generous with rewards, minimally vocal, not revenge-oriented, and willing to relax contribution norms and “spend down” on generosity as the end of the game approaches.

Overall, they exhibit moderate reciprocity and stable alliance-building via reinforcement of trusted cooperators, expressed through behavior rather than overt social talk.
section_text: They receive rewards in several rounds from others, notably from at least one highly generous cooperator. They respond to these rewards primarily through reciprocal action rather than explicit verbal thanks: they keep their contributions high and repeatedly reward that same generous cooperator. Their pattern of rewards appears partially aligned with those who reward them, but not purely reciprocal; they prioritize consistent cooperators even if they are not the largest direct benefactors. They do not gush or engage in emotional gratitude in chat; their style of appreciation is action-based, through continued cooperation and repeated small rewards. When someone complains about not receiving gifts from people, they do not shift their pattern to accommodate that complaint, which suggests they are more driven by their own fairness criteria than by pressure to appease disappointed players. Overall, they exhibit moderate reciprocity and stable alliance-building via reinforcement of trusted cooperators, expressed through behavior rather than overt social talk.

text: They receive rewards repeatedly from others but never explicitly acknowledge or reciprocate them. In chat, they do not thank specific rewarders, do not attempt to form explicit alliances, and do not respond with reciprocal gifts. They also do not adjust their already-maximal contribution behavior in response to being rewarded. This suggests that rewards neither change their strategy nor elicit overt social bonding behavior. Their cooperation appears internally motivated rather than contingent on positive reinforcement, and their reaction to being rewarded is largely neutral or at least behaviorally unchanged. Overall type summary (implicit across sections): This player is a principled, efficiency-oriented unconditional cooperator. They follow a simple internal rule—always contribute maximally to the group good—and do not adapt that rule in response to others’ contributions, punishment, or rewards, nor to the approaching end of the game. They are socially reserved but cooperative in communication, focusing on norm-setting rather than conflict. They avoid both punishment and reward even when group norms push strongly toward their use, and they do not retaliate when harmed or reciprocate when helped. Their incentives appear driven more by a stable internal norm of cooperation and group efficiency than by strategic reciprocity or enforcement concerns.

text: They respond positively but modestly when they receive rewards, acknowledging cooperative allies in chat and expressing appreciation indirectly (e.g., complimenting others or affirming their status as “legends” or similar). Their own reward-giving overlaps with those who reward them or reward others, suggesting a tendency toward indirect and direct reciprocity and alliance reinforcement. However, they do not make explicit “I’ll reward you because you rewarded me” statements; instead, their reciprocity is implied by consistent pro-social targeting and supportive comments. They appear to value being seen as part of the cooperative/reward-giving cluster of players and to strengthen these informal alliances by both words and actions. They do not react with jealousy or resentment when others report higher totals or more gifts; instead, they join light-hearted banter and maintain a friendly group focus.

text: They receive rewards in many rounds but never respond socially or materially: - no chat expressions of thanks or recognition, - no reciprocal rewards to their benefactors, - no special treatment of any specific partner. This indicates either low salience of the gifting mechanism for them, deliberate non-engagement with interpersonal signaling, or a narrow focus on their own contribution rule and final payoff rather than on relational dynamics. They do not attempt to form reciprocal reward loops or alliances. Their cooperative identity is enacted via contributions alone, not via reward reciprocity. Overall, they present as a silent, steadfast, fully cooperative, non-punitive and non-rewarding type, insensitive to social pressure, retaliation, or end-game incentives.

text: Others frequently praise them explicitly for their generosity. They respond in a playful, self‑aware manner, framing their behavior as intentional, generous, and not driven by narrow self‑interest. They do not use this praise to bargain for special treatment, to ask for reciprocal rewards, or to reshape norms; there is no explicit quid‑pro‑quo. Their reward allocations suggest some reciprocity—players who reward them do tend to remain in their positive reward set—but because they reward so widely and consistently, it is unclear whether this is targeted reciprocity or just part of their general prosocial stance. They accept social approval with light humor and continue their existing strategy, using the positive feedback neither to escalate nor to taper their generosity. Overall, they react to being rewarded and praised by maintaining a stable, generous identity rather than by strategically leveraging or modifying alliances. In summary, this player is a “non‑punitive unconditional cooperator and over‑rewarding altruist”: they maximize contributions, eschew punishment entirely, and invest heavily in broad, sometimes indiscriminate positive reinforcement, even at severe personal cost and without end‑game defection. They appear norm‑driven, emotionally resilient to sanctions, and socially warm, preferring to support and reward others rather than control or punish them.

text: They receive rewards multiple times across the game, often at non-trivial levels, but they never acknowledge any specific rewarder in chat and never reciprocate via the reward mechanism. Their only social responses are generic, light emoticons not aimed at particular players, and they do not change their contribution or reward behavior after being rewarded. This indicates that they treat receiving rewards as nice but non-strategic: it does not trigger alliance formation, targeted gratitude, or efforts to pay someone back. They also do not publicly call out or compliment high contributors, even though they are one themselves. Overall, they exhibit low relational reactivity to positive treatment: they keep cooperating at a high level regardless, but they do not build dyadic ties or engage in reciprocity dynamics around rewards.

text: When others reward them, they do not respond with explicit gratitude, direct reciprocation, or targeted alliance-building. They show no observable pattern of increasing contributions immediately after receiving rewards, nor do they start rewarding back. Their social responses around these times are neutral to mildly engaged; they continue chatting in a friendly way but do not signal any special appreciation or reciprocity toward specific rewarders. This suggests that they treat incoming rewards as a bonus windfall rather than as part of a reciprocal relationship that they feel obliged to honor. At the same time, they do not react negatively; they accept the rewards and remain socially pleasant. Overall, their type is “non-reciprocal beneficiary”: they are comfortable receiving rewards, do not punish or reject them, but also do not let them meaningfully alter their strategy or social targeting.

text: They receive nontrivial amounts of reward in many rounds but do not acknowledge specific rewarders by name or attempt to build bilateral alliances. Their contributions remain high both when they are heavily rewarded and when reward to them is low or absent, indicating that their cooperative behavior is not contingent on personal reward receipts. They show no move toward reciprocal rewarding; being rewarded does not trigger “payback” through the reward channel. Instead, their reciprocity appears to operate at the group level: they maintain high contributions as long as the group climate is broadly cooperative, independent of their own direct net transfers. When payoffs drop due to others’ lower contributions, they briefly test defection but do not respond by rewarding compliant players or penalizing noncompliant ones (which is impossible here). Overall, their response to rewarders is muted and indirect: they accept rewards but treat them as incidental rather than as the basis for dyadic relationships or strategy adaptation.

text: They occasionally receive rewards, though much less than they give. When they do, they acknowledge this only indirectly, for example by affirming or engaging positively with the group rather than singling out specific rewarders. There is no explicit verbal gratitude directed to named rewarders, but they respond with supportive chat and continued high contributions and rewards, which can be interpreted as generalized reciprocity toward the group rather than targeted reciprocity to individuals. They do not appear to create explicit alliances or reward cliques around certain players; their reward pattern is broad rather than focused, and their chat addresses the whole group. Their behavior therefore suggests that being rewarded reinforces their already cooperative attitude and their sense of shared experience, but does not noticeably change their strategy: they continue to reward heavily even when personal payoff is negative and even when not being rewarded in return. In summary, they respond to being rewarded by maintaining high prosocial engagement toward the group as a whole, rather than forming narrow reciprocal ties.

text: They receive rewards frequently, especially in the early and middle parts of the game, indicating that others perceive them as a good cooperator or socially valued partner. There is no explicit verbal acknowledgment of specific rewarders, no explicit gratitude, and no clear pattern of targeted reciprocal rewarding that can be inferred from the available data. Their one clear bout of rewarding does not coincide with any direct verbal thanks to named individuals, so whether it is reciprocation is unknown. Socially, however, they respond to being valued in a broader sense: they become an active, humorous member of the group, help sustain positive mood, and continue to contribute highly even after having “earned” a reputation for cooperation. This suggests that the main way they reciprocate social benefits is through continued norm‑consistent behavior and engaging conversation rather than explicit tit‑for‑tat reward exchanges. Their alliance‑building is diffuse and group‑oriented, not dyadic or calculated.

text: They receive rewards fairly consistently, but they do not explicitly express direct gratitude in chat for being rewarded, nor do they name specific rewarders. Their reciprocity is inferred rather than declared: because they say they reward those contributing fully, and because most of the group does so, they likely reward many of the same individuals who reward them, but there is no explicit tit‑for‑tat based on identifiable names. Their reaction is more “role‑based” (reward cooperators) than “person‑based” (reward whoever rewarded me). They do, however, respond socially and playfully to others in chat, including those who visibly take cooperative or generous stances, which can be seen as a softer, non‑monetary form of reciprocity. When someone directly addresses them about the inefficiency of rewards and suggests they stop rewarding that player, they adjust their stated plan but do not entirely abandon rewarding, indicating that they listen but retain their own pro‑social orientation. Overall, their response to being rewarded is muted, non‑transactional, and embedded in a broader, warm group interaction style rather than a strict exchange of favors.

Their pattern of sometimes spending heavily on reward suggests possible indirect reciprocity—rewarding “the group” or cooperators in general—rather than targeted payback to identified individuals, but the data are insufficient to tie their spending to particular earlier donors. They do not attempt to build explicit alliances around reward (“I’ll reward you if you reward me”) and do not comment on receiving more or fewer bonuses over time. Socially, they present as appreciative and engaged with the group overall, but their gratitude is expressed through continued cooperation and occasional reward spending rather than explicit, directed thanks to specific rewarders. Any stronger reciprocity motive remains unknown or, at best, low-confidence.

text: They receive rewards multiple times from others but do not reciprocate with rewards, punishment, or differential contributions. Their behavior remains unchanged: they continue to fully contribute and abstain from all sanctioning. This non-reciprocal stance in the reward dimension suggests they do not use rewards to form coalitions or to express gratitude behaviorally, or they prioritize a simple, stable rule (“always contribute, never sanction”) over contingent reciprocity. They also do not appear to exploit being rewarded by lowering contributions; they maintain their cooperative pattern.

text: They are repeatedly rewarded by others but never reciprocate with rewards. They also do not appear to adjust their punishment behavior to favor those who reward them; they continue to punish some full contributors in spite of the general cooperative climate that includes rewards. This lack of reciprocity suggests that they do not treat rewards as the basis for alliance-building or mutual support. Their behavior implies either low sensitivity to positive social signals or a strategic focus that discounts the value of maintaining rewarding relationships.

text: They receive rewards from others at least once and do not respond by adopting a consistently cooperative stance toward those rewarders. They do not reciprocate with direct rewards back, nor do they clearly increase their own contributions in a sustained way as a response. Any temporary alignment with cooperative behavior does not hold. This suggests that they are relatively unresponsive to positive incentives, at least in a long-run behavioral sense. Alliances based on mutual rewarding do not emerge from their side; they treat rewards more as windfall payoffs than as binding social signals that alter their strategy.

text: This player receives rewards at least once. When they are rewarded while also contributing and punishing defectors, they do not alter their pattern in a new direction but continue and amplify this cooperative norm-enforcement role. They do not appear to reciprocate rewards directly to specific rewarders, but they later extend rewards broadly to the main contributing group, which may reflect a form of generalized reciprocity or coalition-building rather than dyadic payback. Their response to being rewarded is best described as alignment: they behave in ways consistent with the pro-social coalition they are recognized as part of, without clear evidence of targeted reward-for-reward exchange.

This implies that they treat being rewarded more as confirmation that their own cooperative moves are socially approved, rather than as creating an obligation of bilateral reciprocity. Any emotional response (gratitude, increased trust) is unobservable; behaviorally, they remain focused on contribution patterns rather than on dyadic reciprocation of rewards.

text: They receive rewards in multiple instances and can identify who granted them, yet they never reciprocate with explicit rewards to those benefactors. They also do not clearly align their subsequent contribution choices to favor or match the generosity of particular rewarders; their cooperation pattern remains driven by broader group conditions rather than dyadic loyalty. text: They receive rewards regularly but never acknowledge them directly in chat, never reference being rewarded, and never attempt visible strategic reciprocity toward specific rewarders. Their cooperative level is already maximal before and after rewards, so there is no room to “reward” the rewarder by increasing contributions. They also do not switch into using the reward mechanism to reciprocate. Any impact of rewards on their internal feelings is unobserved; behaviorally, they treat rewards as a neutral bonus rather than as a relational signal that calls for a response. They also do not form explicit alliances, do not single out any player as especially good or bad because of rewards, and do not change their tone toward other cooperators versus non-cooperators. This pattern implies that, in this context, they are largely insensitive (in behavioral terms) to reputational or relational aspects of reward, focusing instead on stable cooperation and personal payoff, with gratitude—if present—remaining implicit and unexpressed.
