text: The player receives positive rewards in multiple rounds. However, they never mention being rewarded, never thank any specific rewarder, and never change their contribution pattern in response. Their cooperation level is already high before the first visible reward and remains high in rounds without reward. There is no detectable increase or decrease in cooperation tied to receiving rewards. They also do not appear to form explicit alliances or reciprocate rewards toward those who reward them; they never use the reward mechanism at all, and their chat does not acknowledge any particular player as especially deserving or allied. This indicates that, in this environment, external rewards neither motivate them nor significantly alter their behavior. They appear intrinsically or normatively motivated to contribute, and rewards function as a passive income boost rather than a meaningful social or strategic signal for them.

text: They receive no rewards in the transcript, so their direct response to being rewarded is unknown. Consequently, their propensity for reciprocal rewarding toward specific partners cannot be determined. What can be inferred is that their reward behavior is not driven by having been rewarded themselves, since they continue to spend heavily on others despite never being a beneficiary. This indicates that if they do reciprocate individually in other settings, it is layered on top of a strong baseline of unconditional prosocial rewarding. Their social chat does not single out or praise particular rewarders or high contributors by name, so there is no visible alliance-building or selective gratitude signaling; they behave as a diffuse group-focused benefactor rather than as a coalition strategist.

text: They sometimes receive rewards but never comment on them or try to identify or thank specific rewarders, despite the fact that rewarders are individually visible. There is no observed attempt at direct reciprocity (rewarding back someone who rewarded them) or at forming dyadic alliances. Their single observed act of rewarding comes after a moment of full‑group cooperation, not as a direct response to prior personal reward. This suggests that while they appreciate cooperative behavior in general, they do not strongly differentiate between individuals on the basis of having been rewarded. Being rewarded does not noticeably change their contribution pattern (which is already maxed) or their use of the reward mechanism. Interpersonally, they respond to cooperative conditions with group‑level encouragement (“go team,” “finish strong”) rather than with personalized gratitude or targeted favors. Overall, their reciprocity is diffuse and norm‑based (support the whole cooperative group) rather than partner‑specific (reward those who specifically helped them). They behave as a steadfast, prosocial cooperator who values group welfare, positive atmosphere, and information‑sharing, while treating costly interpersonal reward as an occasional, symbolic extra rather than a core strategic lever.

text: They receive occasional rewards from others, as seen in their outcome summaries, but they do not explicitly acknowledge these rewards in chat. They never name or thank any specific rewarder, never reference having been rewarded, and never appear to reciprocate by rewarding back. Their contributions remain high regardless of being rewarded or not, suggesting that the reward has no observable marginal effect on their cooperative effort. Because the interface makes rewarders identifiable to recipients, their lack of response is informative: they either see rewards as unnecessary, see them but choose not to socially or materially reciprocate, or treat them as incidental noise in an already cooperative environment. There is no visible attempt to build a mutual‑reward “club” or to respond differentially depending on who rewarded them. In a new PGG, this pattern maps onto a type that is largely insensitive—behaviorally and socially—to positive reciprocity in the form of explicit rewards. They maintain cooperation for its own sake or for group‑level payoff reasons, not as a quid‑pro‑quo for being personally rewarded, and they do not invest effort into rewarding back, even when identities are known.

text: The player receives small amounts of reward sporadically but never acknowledges these transfers in chat. There is no evidence of them thanking any specific player, commenting on being rewarded, or altering their behavior to reciprocate toward likely rewarders. Their contribution pattern remains high both when they are rewarded and when they are not, and they still do not spend on rewards after receiving them. This indicates that they do not treat rewards as a salient social signal or as the basis for special alliances. They appear to interpret cooperation as a group-level norm rather than a dyadic exchange: everyone is supposed to contribute fully, and that is its own justification. Being rewarded neither changes their strategy nor prompts overt gratitude; they may notice it privately, but it does not feature in their expressed reasoning or social behavior. In short, they are insensitive, or at least behaviorally unresponsive, to the reward channel, and they neither reward back nor verbally reinforce rewarders.

text: This player is sensitive to being rewarded and appears capable of reciprocating or maintaining cooperative gestures toward those who reward them. When they receive rewards, they do not counter‑punish or ignore the rewarder; instead, their subsequent behavior sometimes involves generous rewarding of others and/or relatively high contributions, consistent with gratitude or at least a desire to sustain positive ties. There is some evidence of generalized rather than strictly directed reciprocity: rather than always returning exactly the same amount to the same individual, they sometimes spread rewards across multiple players, including those who had not just rewarded them. This suggests they may interpret being rewarded as a signal that “rewarding is in play” and then participate in that norm at a group level. At the same time, their reciprocity is not rigid. They also experience rounds where they receive reward but later withhold reward entirely, even when others contribute significantly. This points to a flexible, context‑dependent reciprocity style rather than a tit‑for‑tat rule. They seem willing to reciprocate when it aligns with their broader goals (supporting cooperation, exploring mutual reward), but they can drop reciprocity once it appears unproductive or too costly.

When another player incurs large reward costs to benefit multiple high contributors (including the focal player), the focal player later deviates in contribution (including free-riding) rather than locking into a stable high-cooperation, high-reward exchange with that player. text: When others reward them, this player shows signs of responsiveness but not rigid tit-for-tat reciprocity. They do not ignore rewards, and their later reward allocations are consistent with taking positive treatment into account, yet they do not mechanically return identical amounts to each benefactor. Instead, they seem to integrate rewards into a broader assessment of who is “on their side” and who is contributing cooperatively. They appear open to forming loose, temporary alliances: if another player repeatedly rewards or supports them, they are more likely to direct some rewards back or at least avoid undercutting that partner. However, they still weigh their own payoff and the overall cooperation pattern more heavily than strict bookkeeping of who gave what. Their reciprocity is thus conditional and approximate: they appreciate and sometimes reinforce rewarders, but they do not prioritize exact repayment over strategic flexibility.

text: The player is somewhat responsive but not strongly reciprocal toward those who reward them. When they are rewarded, they do not systematically escalate rewards back to the same individuals in kind. Instead, their own reward expenditures remain modest and do not clearly map one-to-one onto who has been particularly generous to them. This suggests weak direct reciprocity: they notice being rewarded but do not treat it as a binding obligation to match or exceed others’ generosity. They also do not build obvious “alliances” via reward, such as forming a tight reciprocal exchange with a specific partner. Their behavior indicates that they view reward less as a contractual exchange with specific individuals and more as a light, occasional tool for encouraging or acknowledging cooperative norms in the group as a whole. At the same time, they do not retaliate against those who fail to reward them or who reward others more. Given that punishment is unavailable, their only avenue for expressing displeasure would be to withdraw reward from particular partners or sharply reduce contributions. While their contributions sometimes fall after unbalanced reward patterns, there is no clear pattern that they single out or exclude any specific rewarder. This points to a personality that is somewhat independent and self-guided: they appreciate reward but do not anchor their strategy tightly to others’ approval or neglect.

text: The player receives rewards on multiple occasions, and rewarders are identifiable. Following such rewards, their contributions remain generally high or become high again, indicating that being rewarded does not lead to disillusionment or cynicism; instead, it is compatible with continued or renewed cooperation. However, they do not move to an unwaveringly cooperative stance after being rewarded; they still adjust their contributions in response to others’ behavior and overall group patterns. This suggests that reward has a positive but not overriding effect on their decisions. There is evidence that they sometimes respond to being rewarded by later paying to reward others as well. This points toward a tendency for indirect reciprocity: they are willing to “pay forward” cooperation to those who match their cooperative ideals, rather than strictly reciprocating to the same individual in a one‑for‑one manner. Whether they specifically target the same individual(s) who rewarded them is unknown, but their willingness to start or increase rewarding after being rewarded makes a reciprocally oriented interpretation plausible, though with low to moderate confidence. They do not appear to punish or “retaliate” in any way against rewarders (which would be irrational but theoretically observable), and they do not visibly reduce contributions immediately after being rewarded. Instead, their behavior suggests that they interpret rewards as confirmation that cooperative norms are shared by at least some partners. This seems to encourage them to continue experimenting with high contributions and occasional costly rewards, reinforcing a cooperative self‑image and a preference for mutual support rather than strictly maximizing short‑term individual payoff.

There is no clear evidence of forming dyadic alliances based on reward exchanges; the player neither initiates reward relationships nor responds in kind when they receive extra payoff from specific partners. Their high contributions also do not notably increase following receipt of reward, but since contributions are already near the top, it is difficult to infer any incremental motivational effect. Overall, the player behaves as if rewards from others are windfalls that do not change their strategy: they neither “thank” rewarders with counter-rewards, nor visibly adjust cooperation to preferentially favor or disfavor them. This suggests low responsiveness to social incentives when they are mediated by costly side payments, even though the player is fully aware of who has rewarded them. Their reciprocity is thus limited to first-order cooperation (maintaining high contributions), not to second-order reward behavior.

text: When they themselves receive a reward, there is no clear, consistent shift in their use of reward toward the specific rewarder, nor a clear long-term alliance pattern. They do not become a frequent or high-cost rewarder afterward, and they do not obviously target their rewards in a sustained reciprocal fashion. This indicates weak or limited gratitude signaling in behavioral terms, and little evidence of deliberate alliance-building via reward. Their response is more consistent with continuing their prior exploratory strategy than with adopting a reciprocity norm. Any internal feeling of appreciation or trust is unknown; behaviorally, reciprocity appears minimal or absent.

text: When rewarded, this player responds primarily through actions rather than explicit verbal gratitude. They continue to invest in rewarding environments where they are themselves being rewarded and maintain or strengthen ties with highly generous partners. They do not single out rewarders in chat to thank them, but they also do not withdraw from them or retaliate; instead, they keep up a steady stream of moderate rewards, suggesting a tacit expectation of ongoing mutual favor exchange. The absence of explicit thank-yous combined with sustained reciprocal behavior indicates that they treat reward exchanges as part of the “game’s language” rather than as one-off favors needing verbal acknowledgment. They are not purely tit-for-tat, as they sometimes continue rewarding even when immediate reciprocity is weak, but they clearly view rewarders as preferred partners and avoid undermining those relationships.

text: They show some, but not extreme, responsiveness to being rewarded. They continue to contribute generously even when their own incoming rewards are modest or absent, suggesting that their baseline cooperation does not hinge on immediate personal gain from rewarders. At the same time, they reliably keep rewarding players who frequently reward them or other cooperators, which is consistent with indirect reciprocity and alliance maintenance: they appear to pay attention to who behaves pro-socially and reinforce those patterns. There is no explicit verbal gratitude, but materially they often reciprocate or sustain support toward established rewarders. When they are heavily rewarded in a given period, they do not noticeably “withdraw” in subsequent periods; instead, they maintain or increase cooperative behavior, indicating they interpret rewards as validation of a cooperative norm rather than an opportunity to exploit goodwill.

text: When others reward them, they respond socially rather than numerically: they thank people, affirm the relationship, and increase emotional closeness in chat. They make explicit promises to reward particular players after receiving attention from them, and then they honor those promises, signaling a preference for reciprocal, trust‑based ties. Their reciprocity is not perfectly one‑for‑one or strictly calculated, but it is clear that being rewarded moves them to treat those others as allies or favorites. They also maintain positive regard even toward rewarders who later reduce transfers, not shifting into hostility or retaliation. Overall, they treat reward as a social signal: receiving it strengthens bonds and encourages them to keep rewarding and supporting those partners across rounds.

text: They receive frequent and often sizable rewards from multiple others. Despite this, they never reciprocate with rewards, even toward those who obviously focus their reward spending on them. They also never acknowledge rewarders in chat. This indicates low behavioral reciprocity in the reward dimension, even under clear, identifiable conditions. text: They clearly notice when they are rewarded and respond in both chat and behavior. They explicitly thank others, and their tone is warm and appreciative. After receiving affection and support in chat, they often increase or maintain their own generosity through rewards, sometimes directing rewards toward those who have treated them kindly. This indicates social reciprocity: they are sensitive to kindness, both in rewards and in words, and try to “give back” in a diffuse way. However, their responses are not strictly dyadic; rather than targeting only one benefactor, they often spread rewards among several cooperative or friendly players, which suggests a generalized reciprocity style more than narrow repayment. They appear motivated by belonging and positive group sentiment: praise and inclusion from others coincide with them reaffirming the relationship verbally and through costly reward. They do not retaliate against non-rewarders; the absence of punishment and the lack of negative messaging toward those who fail to reward them show that they do not enforce reciprocity in a harsh or punitive way.

There is no observable behavioral change in their contribution level because they are already at maximum cooperation before and after. text: This player is rewarded once by another participant and does not respond by changing their own behavior in any observable way. They continue to contribute fully, exactly as before, and do not begin rewarding others in return. There is no evidence of material reciprocity through the reward mechanism, alliance-building via selective rewarding, or retaliation/non-retaliation decisions (since no one punishes them). Their cooperative behavior is already at ceiling, so no upward adjustment is possible in contributions. The key observation is that they do not repay the reward with a reward, despite knowing that rewarders are identifiable. This suggests that their incentive use is not driven by reciprocity norms in this context; they maintain their existing policy of not using reward, rather than adapting it to reciprocate favor. This pattern is consistent with a player who cooperates out of an internal rule or moral standard, but does not layer on additional strategic or relational gestures via side payments. They may privately feel gratitude (unknown), but behaviorally they remain unchanged: a stable, non-signaling full cooperator.

text: They respond to being rewarded with explicit thanks and positive emotive reactions. When another player gives them coins, they acknowledge it by name and express gratitude, and they also verbally thank others who are publicly generous (e.g., someone else who is distributing rewards). However, there is no clear one-to-one “you rewarded me, so I reward you back” cycle; their reciprocity is primarily verbal and relational rather than mechanical. They sometimes reward players who have helped keep the cooperative norm strong or who are socially central, which may reflect a diffuse reciprocity and alliance-building tendency rather than direct quid pro quo. Overall, they are appreciative, friendly, and reinforcing when rewarded but do not use reward strictly as a transactional exchange. --- Type label (for use elsewhere): “Warm Unconditional Cooperator” Core type summary: This player behaves as a principled, group-oriented cooperator who commits to full contributions regardless of others’ behavior or the game’s remaining length. They avoid punishment altogether, occasionally pay to reward others as a social and appreciative gesture, and maintain a consistently warm, affirming communication style. Their strategy is simple and norm-driven rather than adaptive: keep contributing maximally, support a positive group atmosphere, and express gratitude when others act kindly. They show high tolerance for free-riding and end-game defection, placing social harmony and cooperative ideals above individual payoff optimization.

section_text: They explicitly notice and acknowledge when others reward them, thanking the rewarders in chat. While they do not always directly reciprocate one-to-one, they do later engage in very broad generosity toward the group, which can be interpreted as generalized reciprocity: kindness from others reinforces their inclination to be kind to everyone. Their thanks are warm but not effusive; they integrate these social exchanges into the cooperative group narrative (“thanks,” “ty”) rather than escalating them into exclusive alliances. text: Because rewarders are identifiable, this player could, in principle, build targeted alliances. However, there is no clear evidence that they condition their contribution level on being rewarded, or that they selectively reward specific generous partners more than others in a way that visibly changes their own cooperative behavior. They continue to reward even in periods when they personally receive relatively little or no reward, indicating that their willingness to reward is not tightly reciprocally contingent. They do not appear to withdraw reward as retaliation for being under-rewarded, nor do they dramatically escalate their own contribution in response to being especially favored. This implies that their response to reward is relatively muted in strategic terms: they appreciate and accept it (unknown psychologically), but behaviorally they largely stick to their own pre-set patterns. There is weak evidence of any strong dyadic alliance: their contributions stay near their anchor across time and they do not pivot into clearly higher contributions toward a particular cooperator, even when some peers consistently contribute more. If there is any reciprocity toward rewarders, it is subtle and not dominant in shaping their choices. Overall, this player behaves as a “system-level reward supporter” more than a partner-level reciprocator: they reward to sustain general cooperation rather than because particular individuals have rewarded them, and their own cooperative level is only weakly sensitive to who has rewarded them previously.

text: Because detailed, individual-level reward flows are not observable from the transcript, the precise direction of reciprocity toward specific rewarders is unknown. However, the aggregate pattern suggests that the player does not condition their generosity strongly on how much reward they personally receive. They continue to reward substantially even when the net reward they receive is low or absent, and they do not obviously downshift their cooperative or rewarding behavior following episodes of weak reciprocation. This implies a low sensitivity to short-term inequity in the reward space and a tendency toward unconditional or weakly conditional positive reciprocity. If anything, their behavior is consistent with trying to sustain or repair cooperation through continued generosity rather than retaliating or withdrawing support when others fail to reward them. They may notice differences in others’ rewarding, but there is no sign that they form a tight “reward alliance” with particular partners or engage in tit-for-tat style mutual reward escalation; their pattern looks more group-focused than dyad-focused. Overall, they present as a prosocial, norm-promoting type who treats rewarding and contributing as part of a stable cooperative identity, only weakly modulated by how generously others reward them.

text: The player reliably receives rewards when they conform to or slightly exceed group contribution norms, but this does not elicit a clear behavioral alliance with likely rewarders. Being rewarded does not lead to a transition into systematic over-contribution, nor does it prompt a shift into active rewarding. Their contribution pattern continues to be driven more by group averages and payoff context than by apparent reciprocity toward specific individuals. They do not show targeted, costly reciprocation toward any identifiable cooperative partner; instead, their response to being rewarded is primarily behavioral stability: they maintain generally cooperative levels, but do not escalate to “partner favoritism.” This suggests that they interpret rewards more as confirmation that their baseline cooperative strategy is acceptable, rather than as an invitation into a special mutual-support pact. Personality-wise, this points to a pragmatic cooperator: they appreciate and respond to the incentive structure at the level of contribution decisions but largely ignore the second-order reward channel as a tool for relationship-building. Their reciprocity is mostly first-order (matching group cooperation) rather than second-order (rewarding rewarders). Whether they feel internal gratitude but suppress it for cost reasons, or they are simply indifferent to symbolic reciprocity, is unknown.

text: They respond to being rewarded by staying cooperative and continuing to invest in rewarding others, rather than by pulling back to exploit the goodwill. Receiving reward does not trigger observable revenge, spite, or opportunistic free-riding; instead, it is consistent with a pattern of sustained cooperation and high reward spending. Because rewarders are identifiable, they can in principle build alliances. The data show that they often receive a nontrivial amount of reward across many rounds, implying that at least some peers recognize and appreciate their behavior. Their subsequent behavior—maintaining or increasing reward expenditures while keeping their contribution rule intact—suggests a tendency toward reciprocity in a broad sense: they uphold and reinforce a cooperative climate in which mutual rewarding is normal. However, there is no clear evidence that they tailor their contributions specifically to individual rewarders as opposed to the group as a whole; their contributions remain highly regular regardless of how much they are rewarded. Their main adjustment channel appears to be in the reward stage rather than in the contribution stage. When they receive more reward, they do not visibly “cash in” by decreasing their own giving. Instead, they continue to bear high reward costs, which looks like commitment to a cooperative identity and possibly gratitude expressed through ongoing support of others. In social/personality terms, they resemble a “steadfast positive reciprocator”: they interpret reward as confirmation that cooperative norms are shared and respond by continuing to invest in those norms. They are not strongly vindictive or opportunistic; their reactions to being rewarded are aligned with maintaining a friendly, mutually supportive group environment rather than extracting maximum private benefit.

This suggests conditional, though not meticulously tracked, reciprocity: they are attentive to who is “good” (high contributors, possibly rewarders) and are inclined to “have their back” later. Their ongoing high contributions and continued reward spending despite only modest incoming rewards indicate that their cooperative motivation is not solely tit-for-tat; they seem more driven by a general prosocial norm and group identity than by strict dyadic bookkeeping. When rewarded, they neither shift behavior toward exploitation (no reduction in contributions) nor express entitlement; instead, they maintain the same cooperative stance, using light humor and social bonding rather than explicit gratitude rituals. Overall, they respond to rewarders with goodwill and occasional targeted reciprocity, embedded in a broader, group-focused cooperative style rather than in narrow alliance-building.

text: They receive rewards multiple times but never comment on them directly in chat—no explicit thanks, no attempt to identify specific rewarders, and no pledge of reciprocity. Their contributions remain maxed out regardless of being rewarded, so there is no visible behavioral change that can be attributed to gratitude or alliance-building. When they ask whether anyone is rewarding others and hear that some are, they still do not begin rewarding themselves, suggesting low reciprocity through the formal mechanism. Their “reciprocity,” if any, is expressed only through continued high cooperation and light-hearted social engagement, not through material transfers. Overall, they appear largely indifferent to the reward system as a channel for social bonding or strategic alliances, treating rewards as a pleasant but non-central side effect rather than something that reshapes their decisions.

text: This player receives rewards in multiple rounds, sometimes substantial amounts. They never complain about not being rewarded, nor do they demand recognition. When they do respond to reward‑related dynamics, it is subtle and relational rather than transactional. They acknowledge reciprocity toward at least one specific peer by later mentioning that they had given that peer something, which can be interpreted as a weak form of reciprocal favor or alliance signaling. However, they do not overtly build a visible “reward clique” in chat and do not use reward to negotiate future behavior. Their public reaction to being rewarded is effectively neutral or folded into general camaraderie; they maintain friendly banter and affectionate closings near the end rather than explicit thanks or strategic bargaining. Overall, their response to being rewarded is consistent with a cooperative but not hyper‑calculating type: they appreciate and sometimes reciprocate, but they do not turn rewards into explicit contracts or long‑term alliances, and they keep the tone light rather than instrumental.

text: They receive rewards in multiple rounds and, at least once, publicly acknowledge that they are rewarding others. There is no explicit verbal expression of gratitude toward specific rewarders in the chat, and no stated commitment to reciprocate. However, they do increase their reward spending in several later rounds relative to early ones, and they keep rewarding even when they are not currently being heavily rewarded, which may indirectly reflect a norm of mutual aid with other rewarders. Because the transcript does not identify who rewards them, it is unknown whether they systematically reciprocate to particular individuals. Their pattern is more consistent with generalized reciprocity or group-focused redistribution than with dyadic alliance-building. They do not attempt to coordinate “reward circles” in chat, nor do they explicitly promise rewards in exchange for behaviors. In summary, when rewarded, they continue or escalate their own rewarding rather than withdrawing; they appear broadly pro-reward and willing to sustain a generous stance without demanding explicit, targeted reciprocity. Their response is cooperative in terms of the reward mechanism, but only weakly conditioned on identifiable partners, with specific reciprocation patterns remaining unknown.

text: They never explicitly mention being rewarded or name any specific rewarder in the chat, so their verbal response to identifiable rewarders is unknown. They do, however, acknowledge at least one player’s promise to “get them next time” with a light, friendly exchange later, which may indicate openness to reciprocal goodwill, but the link to actual reward behavior cannot be firmly established. They respond politely when others direct friendly comments to them and offer a general “thank you” near the end when players express appreciation to the group, which can be interpreted as diffuse gratitude rather than targeted reciprocity. Their own heavy reward spending does not obviously depend on whether they themselves were rewarded in the immediately preceding round; some of their largest outflows occur even when their own inflows are small. This pattern suggests low conditional reciprocity and a more unconditional, principle-based generosity rather than a strict “you rewarded me, so I reward you” strategy. Overall, their stance toward rewarders is cooperative and appreciative in tone but not clearly contingent; alliance-building is possible but not demonstrable from the available evidence (low confidence).

text: Their response to receiving rewards is purely behavioral in terms of payoff; there is no social or strategic acknowledgement in chat. They are rewarded multiple times while contributing little or nothing, yet they do not publicly thank anyone, do not attempt to identify or ally with generous rewarders, and do not visibly adjust their contributions upward in a consistent way in response. They also do not engage in directed reciprocal rewarding. Even after receiving sizeable rewards, they do not start spending their own coins to give back to the group or to specific players. There is no evidence that they treat reward as a social signal that should be reciprocated; instead, they behave as a free-rider on both the contribution and reward systems. Thus, in environments where rewarders are identifiable, this player tends not to build alliances, coordinate with them, or protect them. They accept the material benefits without forming observable reciprocal obligations. Their type is best described as a low-reciprocity, low-gratitude beneficiary of others’ generosity, focused on personal earnings rather than relationship management.

text: They are repeatedly rewarded by others, including in situations where their own contributions are minimal, yet they never alter their behavior in a direction that would signal gratitude or reciprocity. There is no increase in their contributions following being rewarded, and no deployment of rewards in return. They also never send chat messages acknowledging or thanking specific rewarders, even though they can see who rewarded them. This pattern fits a type that treats incoming rewards as windfall gains rather than as part of a reciprocal relationship. They neither reciprocate directly to the rewarders nor indirectly by improving their cooperation level. From the outside, they present as unresponsive to positive incentives at the social level (thanks, alliances, mutual recognition) and only responsive to personal payoff totals. Whether they consciously exploit rewarders or simply disregard the social meaning of rewards is unknown, but behaviorally they function as a non-reciprocating beneficiary of others’ generosity.

Given their own rare but targeted use of rewards and their non-retaliatory style in punishment, a plausible—but low-confidence—inference is that they would treat rewards similarly to punishment: as a signal about norms and expectations rather than as an invitation to personal alliance or tit-for-tat exchange. However, their actual reciprocity to rewarders remains unknown.

text: They receive rewards in multiple rounds from several different players. Despite that, they never overtly acknowledge or thank rewarders in chat and do not clearly form an exclusive reciprocal-reward dyad or alliance. When they reward, it is not obviously targeted only at those who have rewarded them before; their spending occurs in both earlier and later phases and seems more tied to current cooperative levels than to strict tit-for-tat reciprocity. This indicates low explicit gratitude signaling and low strategic alliance-building via the reward channel. If reciprocity is present, it is weak or indirect, and there is no evidence they attempt to “trade” rewards or build a coalition of mutual rewarders. Their response to being rewarded is primarily behavioral inertia: they continue full cooperation regardless of being rewarded or ignored.

text: When other players reward them, they do not show clear, systematic reciprocal rewarding toward those specific individuals. They do not shift into a visible “you reward me, I reward you” bilateral alliance. Instead, their reward decisions continue to track contribution behavior more than prior rewards received. They also do not comment in chat when rewarded, so they offer no verbal gratitude or alliance signaling. Their reciprocity is thus weak in a direct, dyadic sense: they do not obviously “pay back” rewarders as such. However, receiving reward does not make them more selfish; their contribution and reward patterns remain generally cooperative after being rewarded, implying that they take reward as supportive but do not reorient their strategy around it. Overall, their response to reward is muted and behaviorally principled: they remain focused on contribution-based norms, not on personal loyalty or tit-for-tat rewarding. Any subtle shifts toward rewarders are too small or inconsistent to infer with confidence.

text: They receive rewards multiple times and at least once in a very large amount from a particular co-player. Their behavioral response to being rewarded is muted and largely non-communicative: they do not verbally thank, praise, or explicitly coordinate with rewarders. There is no visible attempt to form an open alliance in chat or to acknowledge the favor socially. In terms of behavior, their subsequent contributions do not shift into full, consistent high cooperation, nor do they become a heavy rewarder in return. They sometimes maintain or slightly adjust contributions but do not lock into a sustained reciprocation pattern. They do occasionally reward others in later rounds, including some who have rewarded them previously or who are strong cooperators, which might indicate weak, indirect reciprocity, but this is low-confidence; the data are compatible with them simply rewarding based on contribution levels rather than on personal loyalty. Overall, they treat incoming rewards more as a bonus than as an obligation-creating signal. They are not overtly ungrateful, but their reciprocity is limited and mostly implicit: they do not seem to feel strong social pressure to pay back rewarders in kind.

text: The player receives reward multiple times from several different others, including when they contribute fully and also when they are free-riding. They never explicitly acknowledge reward in chat and do not clearly reciprocate through targeted rewarding of those same individuals. They do not develop visible dyadic alliances: there is no pattern where receiving reward from a specific player reliably leads to this player rewarding that partner later. Nor is there a clear behavioral shift toward higher contributions after being rewarded; their contribution levels remain volatile and often revert to low, suggesting that they treat incoming reward as a windfall rather than a signal to change strategy. In terms of reciprocity, they show at most weak, indirect responsiveness. They sometimes reward in periods where generous behavior by others is evident, but since they reward so rarely and non-specifically, this looks more like occasional generalized reciprocity or experimentation than deliberate payback to identifiable rewarders.

text: Their behavior when rewarded does not show clear, consistent direct reciprocity toward specific rewarders. They do receive rewards multiple times and their subsequent actions sometimes include higher contributions, but these higher contributions also align with broader shifts in group cooperation. Because they never explicitly acknowledge reward in chat, their internal attribution (gratitude vs. instrumental expectation of future gains) is unknown. They do not obviously engage in symmetric payback—there is no systematic pattern of rewarding or especially favoring those who have rewarded them in prior rounds. Their occasional rewards tend to go to highly cooperative players regardless of whether those players were recent rewarders of them personally. This suggests that their response to being rewarded is indirect: feeling part of a cooperative climate may make them more willing to contribute and occasionally to reward high contributors, but they do not treat reward relationships as dyadic alliances. Overall, they seem to interpret reward primarily as a group-level signal of cooperation viability rather than as an interpersonal bond that must be repaid. Their reciprocity is therefore weak at the individual level and stronger at the conditional-cooperation level (“if the group is generous/cooperative, I can be too”), with the specific identity of the rewarder playing a minor or unknown role.

text: The player is rewarded at various points. When rewarded, they do not settle into a stable, sustained high-contribution strategy in response. Sometimes they later contribute a lot and sometimes a little, with no obvious long-run upward shift attributable to having been rewarded. Thus, they show limited behavioral compliance with reward as an incentive to maintain high cooperation. Their sanctioning of rewarders is not consistently favorable. They sometimes continue to punish or withhold rewards from others who have rewarded them previously, indicating that they do not treat receipt of rewards as the basis for a stable alliance or exclusive reciprocity. Any reciprocity that exists is weak or short-lived: they may respond with some rewards back to high cooperators who also reward them, but over time their punish/reward pattern remains complex and not tightly locked into reciprocal dyads. Receiving rewards does not noticeably dampen their willingness to punish those same individuals later, suggesting that they value their own right to evaluate and sanction each round independently more than maintaining strict loyalty to past benefactors. Overall, their response to reward: limited and inconsistent reciprocity, no strong alliance-building, and only weak, short-term sensitivity to positive reinforcement.

text: The player receives rewards on a few occasions and does not ignore them at the behavioral level. After being rewarded once following a very high contribution, they sharply reduce contributions in the next decision, suggesting that reward does not function as a straightforward encouragement to continue high cooperation. The immediate pattern is more consistent with opportunism (“I’ve been treated well, I can now afford to take more for myself”) or with experimentation about how far they can lower contributions while maintaining acceptable treatment. Later, receiving a smaller reward is followed by maintaining or restoring relatively high contributions rather than by immediate exploitation. This indicates that the player does not have a fixed rule like “reward ⇒ defect next” or “reward ⇒ cooperate more.” Instead, they appear to treat reward as one of many signals in a noisy environment and continue to adjust in a mixed way, sometimes cooperating, sometimes exploiting. They do not reciprocate rewards with rewards of their own, nor do they build explicit “alliances” via incentive use. Any reciprocity they express is limited to their own contribution choices and remains partial and unstable. Overall, they respond to rewards primarily in terms of their private strategy testing and payoff-seeking, rather than as emotionally driven gratitude or loyalty.

text: This player is repeatedly rewarded by others but does not show a clear, consistent adjustment in contribution or sanctioning behavior in response. There is no obvious shift toward aligning with the apparent preferences of whoever rewards them, and they do not form a stable “mutual reward” pattern with any specific peer that we can infer. They sometimes increase contributions after being rewarded and sometimes reduce them; they also alternate between punishing and rewarding peers independent of prior rewards they themselves receive. This suggests that gratitude-based reciprocity is weak or absent in their observable behavior. They do not treat rewards as a strong relational signal requiring reciprocation through targeted reward or cooperative alignment. Overall, their response to being rewarded appears muted and idiosyncratic: they continue to experiment with contributions, punishment, and reward toward both peers without clearly privileging any identifiable rewarder. They behave as though others’ rewards are background noise rather than a central input into their strategy.

They appear unmoved by attempts to build a positive relationship or to encourage them into cooperation through selective rewarding. Their contribution rule remains fixed at zero, and they do not engage in any visible alliance-building. This indicates an absence of reciprocity toward benefactors in this setting. text: The player is rewarded by others in some rounds and does not materially change their strategy in response: they already cooperate at a stable level and continue to do so. They do not systematically ramp up contributions following rewards, suggesting that their cooperative baseline is not heavily contingent on receiving positive reinforcement. Likewise, they do not start rewarding new individuals just because those individuals rewarded them; there is no clear pattern of direct reciprocal reward-for-reward behavior. Instead, their reward targeting remains mostly aligned with contribution levels. This implies that they value reward but do not treat it as a basis for tight reciprocal alliances. Their response is best described as steady, independent cooperation with mild gratitude that is expressed indirectly (by sustaining their existing reward and contribution norms) rather than through explicit reciprocal favor-trading. Overall, they behave as a stable, intrinsically cooperative rewarder whose strategy is only weakly modulated by the rewards they personally receive.

text: They repeatedly receive some reward from others, but the amounts are small or inconsistent relative to how much they themselves spend. There is no clear behavioral shift toward more self-protection after being under-rewarded; they maintain both high contributions and high reward outlays even when they are scarcely rewarded or not rewarded at all. This suggests they do not strongly condition their cooperative or reward behavior on personal reward income. Their stance appears closer to unconditional or weakly conditional cooperation with weak personal reciprocity: they do not “retaliate” when others fail to reward them, nor do they clearly scale up rewards selectively for those who reward them. Instead, they seem to treat rewarding as a general tool to sustain or endorse group cooperation, not as a narrow tit-for-tat with particular rewarders. Their sensitivity to being rewarded emotionally or strategically is unknown, but their continued generosity despite low returns implies low dependence on direct reciprocity and a willingness to accept asymmetric exchanges.

text: They are rewarded a few times by others. text: They receive some rewards and respond appreciatively. When another player gifts back to them after they punished, they acknowledge it and accept it without resentment. They thank or respond warmly when recognized, and when an intended exchange partner confirms reciprocation, they answer in a friendly way and propose further coordinated exchanges. They appear comfortable with forming a small alliance around reciprocal gifting, and they view those who reward them as partners rather than as manipulators or threats. Even after the group concludes that gifting is inefficient, they maintain positive relations with prior reward partners and include them in plans for possible final-round gifting to help those who are below thresholds. There is no evidence of them punishing or withdrawing cooperation from past rewarders; instead, they signal an intention to repay generosity indirectly (e.g., by planning to gift to “whomever needs it,” including previously generous players), consistent with a reciprocal-prosocial orientation, albeit constrained by their own threshold pursuit.

text: When others reward them, the player notices, expresses thanks, and sometimes reciprocates indirectly. They publicly thank people for giving, and they explicitly acknowledge a partner’s suggestion of mutual gifting, later saying they “gave it back,” indicating a willingness to reciprocate when they perceive help or coordinated transfers. They openly appreciate generosity from others and join in praising particularly generous players. They also later report and discuss their own coin total relative to others, suggesting a sense of shared progress and mutual support. However, once they internalize the inefficiencies of gifting, they move away from encouraging ongoing reward exchanges for everyone, and their reciprocity is primarily symbolic or targeted rather than systematic. Overall, their response to rewarders is prosocial and appreciative: they are emotionally responsive to kindness, willing to reciprocate or at least thank publicly, and they integrate that into a broader cooperative identity without letting it fully override efficiency once the structure is understood.

text: They show clear positive emotional responses to being rewarded, often thanking specific players by name. When others gift to them or announce plans to help them hit a threshold, they respond with appreciation and modesty, and at times with self-deprecating comments about having “given it all away.” They do not immediately shift to hoarding after being helped; instead, they often continue gifting, which indicates that gratitude does not translate into self-protection but into ongoing generosity. There is some indirect reciprocity: they repeatedly reward those who are themselves generous or who publicly commit to helping others, including them. However, this reciprocity is diffuse and not strictly tit-for-tat. Their responses build a sense of alliance and mutual goodwill rather than bilateral trading relationships. Overall, they interpret rewards as social signals of kindness and respond with warmth, increased attachment to the group, and continued prosocial behavior, even when this is costly.

text: They do receive some rewards from others, but they never comment on them in chat. There is no explicit verbal gratitude, coordination attempt, or alliance-building based on being rewarded. Behaviorally, their later rewards do not show clear one-to-one reciprocity toward specific previous rewarders; instead, their recipient choices span multiple different co-players. This suggests that they treat reward more as general encouragement of cooperators than as dyadic reciprocity. Because they remain highly cooperative throughout regardless of who rewards or punishes them, it is unclear whether being rewarded meaningfully changes their strategy; at most, it might modestly reinforce an already strong preference for full contribution. Their lack of explicit social signaling in response to rewards indicates a low-communication, low-alliance style: they accept rewards passively and continue their preferred cooperative behavior, without turning it into a naming, bonding, or trading process. Overall, they are a quietly cooperative, weakly reciprocal, non-punitive contributor whose main consistent trait is to keep giving fully to the group, largely independent of direct personal treatment.

These rewards do not induce any new behaviors: they do not begin rewarding in return, do not start punishing non-cooperators, and do not shift their contribution pattern beyond what they are already doing. Their contributions are already at ceiling when most rewards arrive, so there is little room for upward adjustment; their strategy remains stable rather than becoming more strategic or alliance-focused. They appear to accept rewards passively without expressing reciprocity via the mechanism. This suggests limited use of the reward structure for coalition-building or tit-for-tat reciprocity; their cooperative stance is largely one-way and norm-based rather than contingent on being thanked or supported.

text: The player is rewarded in some rounds but does not alter their strategy in any clear, new direction afterward: they were already a high contributor and remain so, and they still do not start rewarding others. They also do not appear to grant “leniency” to the chronic defector following reward from any party; their punishment pattern continues to focus on that defector according to their own fairness standard. Because identities of specific rewarders are knowable in this setup, their lack of behavioral shift suggests limited reciprocity toward rewarders, at least in terms of using the costly mechanisms. They neither build observable alliances via reward nor reciprocate with counter-reward. Their response to being rewarded is best characterized as neutral: it neither provokes gratitude expressed via costly acts, nor retaliation, nor any noticeable change in contributions, which were already at ceiling. The player thus appears relatively insensitive, behaviorally, to positive incentives directed at them, at least within the bounds of this game.

text: They repeatedly receive rewards from others and never respond with punishment or decreased cooperation. After being rewarded, they sometimes reward others broadly, but this is also consistent with their general pro-reward stance, so direct reciprocity strength is uncertain. They do not appear to form narrow alliances based on mutual rewarding; instead, their rewards go to nearly all high contributors, including those who have not especially favored them. This suggests that, although they may feel some gratitude, their main reference for rewarding is contribution behavior, not who rewarded them personally. Their cooperation does not increase noticeably after being rewarded because it is already very high; they maintain their established cooperative baseline rather than adjusting upward. Overall, they are compatible with a “reciprocal in spirit, but principle-driven” responder: open to positive cycles of reward but guided more by norms than by dyadic tit-for-tat.

text: When others reward them, they do not alter their contribution (which remains full) and do not systematically reciprocate reward to particular individuals. Their later rewards are often distributed broadly across all high contributors rather than targeted reciprocation to their past benefactors. This indicates that they do not see reward as the basis of bilateral alliances but rather as a tool for public norm reinforcement. They are not obviously “loyal” in a dyadic sense; instead, they remain loyal to the general cooperation standard. Receiving reward has little visible effect on their sanctioning pattern: they continue to punish under‑contributors (even if those under‑contributors sometimes spend heavily to reward others) and to spare consistent full contributors, regardless of private gifting directed at them.

text: They receive rewards from others multiple times and do not reciprocate in a one-to-one, tit-for-tat fashion with specific individuals; instead, they respond at the group level. Their later reward patterns favor all high contributors rather than only those who previously rewarded them. This indicates a generalized reciprocity or group-norm focus rather than dyadic alliance building. They do not “buy” alliances by narrowly rewarding mutual rewarders; instead, they maintain an inclusive criterion: sustained high contribution. The absence of targeted reciprocity suggests they interpret incoming rewards as confirmation of a cooperative group norm rather than as a personalized exchange that must be balanced.

text: They are rewarded multiple times by other players, often when they contribute fully or behave cooperatively. Their behavioral response to being rewarded is to continue or return to high contributions and, in many instances, to also reward others in subsequent phases. They do not appear to reciprocate reward in a targeted, one-to-one manner (e.g., rewarding only those who rewarded them); instead, they tend to reward all high contributors broadly. This suggests that they interpret being rewarded as affirmation of a group norm and respond by reinforcing that norm with generalized reciprocity rather than direct tit-for-tat. They do not reduce contributions or switch to punishment in direct response to being rewarded; rather, reward coincides with or precedes periods of strong group cooperation and broad mutual reinforcement. Their reaction can be characterized as “norm-solidifying” rather than “personal alliance building”: they treat reward exchanges as a group-level relationship rather than dyadic alliances with specific individuals.

text: The player receives rewards on many occasions from multiple others. They do not visibly alter their contribution pattern in response, because they already contribute at the maximum level before and after receiving rewards. They also do not form obvious exclusive reward partnerships; although they sometimes reward players who reward them, they also reward others who do not, and they do not systematically match the intensity of any particular rewarder. Their reciprocity is thus weakly targeted: they behave in a generally prosocial way rather than forming tight bilateral alliances. There is no evidence of dropping contributions or rewards in response to withdrawing rewards from others; their cooperative behavior is stable and not contingent on being appreciated. Their type is “reward-responsive but not reward-dependent”: they remain fully cooperative regardless of how much they are personally rewarded, and their own rewarding is guided more by group cooperation patterns than by strict tit-for-tat.
